items.title: Promote a notebook helper into a real CLI
items.scenario: You keep re-running `chunk_text(open('doc.txt').read(), 512)` in different notebooks.
items.explanation: Wrap it as `ragapp chunk doc.txt --max-tokens 512`. The CLI module just parses, calls `chunk_text`, prints chunks. Notebooks now `from ragapp.chunker import chunk_text`; the shell uses the CLI. Same logic, two interfaces.

items.title: Sub-commands for a multi-step pipeline
items.scenario: Your RAG pipeline has stages: ingest, index, search.
items.explanation: Use sub-commands: `ragapp ingest ./docs`, `ragapp index --backend faiss`, `ragapp search 'query'`. Each is its own handler in `mypkg.cli.commands`; the top-level parser just routes. Looks like `git`, scales nicely.

items.title: Read stdin with `-` convention
items.scenario: You want `cat doc.txt | ragapp chunk -` to work like every Unix tool.
items.explanation: If `args.file == '-'`, read from `sys.stdin`. Otherwise read from the path. One line of code; instant pipeline compatibility.

items.title: Exit codes for CI gating
items.scenario: Your CLI runs a validator; CI should fail if validation finds issues.
items.explanation: Return `0` if clean, `1` if validation errors, `2` for usage errors (argparse handles this). CI uses `set -e`; non-zero stops the build. Don't print error messages to stdout — use stderr.

items.title: Reuse CLI logic from another Python program
items.scenario: A scheduled job needs to do the same thing as `ragapp index`.
items.explanation: If the CLI is thin, the job imports `from ragapp.index import build_index` and calls it directly — no subprocess, no argv juggling. The CLI and the job both call the same function.
